<!--!!! THIS FILE IS AUTO-GENERATED. DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY. EDIT THE FILES IN THE SRCQMD DIR !!!-->

# The numbers

## Introduction

Arabic numbers are complicated and we will treat them more fully in chapter\ (give ref),
if [#allAh]{.trn2} wills.
For now, we will give a short preview of the numbers from one to ten.

## The number one

The word for the number "one" is
[وَاحِد]{.ar} [wAHid]{.trn}.
It is an adjectival noun and can be used as a attribute.
For example,

[قَرَأْتُ كِتَابًا وَاحِدًا.]{.ar}  
"I read one book."

As usual, when the described noun is feminine, then the attribute too [وَاحِد]{.ar} is feminized:

[أَكَلْتُ تَمْرَةً وَاحِدَةً.]{.ar}  
"I ate one date."

Often, as in English, using the number 
[وَاحِد]{.ar} "one" is optional.
For example,

[قَرَأْتُ كِتَابً.]{.ar}  
"I read a book."

## The number two

The number for "one"
[وَاحِد]{.ar}
is a singular noun.
Correspondingly, the number for "two" 
[ٱثْنَانِ]{.ar} [ivnAni]{.trn}
is a dual noun.
It too can be used as attribute.
For example,

[دَخَلَ رَجْلَانِ ٱثْنَانِ.]{.ar}  
[daxala rajulAni -vnAn]{.trn}  
"Two men entered."

In the the a-case and i-case, it becomes
[ٱثْنَيْنِ]{.ar} [ivnayni]{.trn}.
For example,

[قَرَأْتُ كِتَابَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ.]{.ar}  
[qaraEtu kitAbayni -vnayn]{.trn}  
"I read two books."

When feminized it becomes
[ٱثْنَتَانِ]{.ar} [ivnatAni]{.trn}.
For example,

[دَخَلَتْ جَارِيَتَانِ ٱثْنَتَانِ وَأَكَلَتَا تَمْرَتَيْنِ ٱثْنَتَيْنِ.]{.ar}  
[daxala jAriyatAni -vnatAni waEakalatA tamratayni -vnatayn.]{.trn}  
"Two girls entered and ate two dates."

As with
[وَاحِد]{.ar}
"one",
the use of the number two is optional because its meaning is inherent in the dual.
For example,

[دَخَلَتْ جَارِيَتَانِ وَأَكَلَتَا تَمْرَتَيْنِ.]{.ar}  
[daxala jAriyatAni waEakalatA tamratayn.]{.trn}  
"Two girls entered and ate two dates."

## The numbers from three to ten

We saw that the number
[وَاحِد]{.ar}
"one"
is a singular noun, and that the number
[ٱثْنَانِ]{.ar} [ivnAni]{.trn}
"two"
is a dual noun.

So it should come as no surprise that the numbers three to ten are treated as plural nouns.
(We say _treated as plurals_ because they're not technically plurals.
For example, they don't conform to the broken plural patterns.
But let's not worry about that for now.)

Now, remember that [ة]{.ar} is a feminine marker for singular nouns, but not for plural nouns.
In fact, recall that there is almost a role reversal for [ة]{.ar} in plural nouns, where it tends to occur for plurals of masculine nouns, and is missing for plurals of feminine nouns.
For example, the plural of [هِرّ]{.ar} "a male cat" is [هِرَرَة]{.ar},
and the plural of [هِرَّة]{.ar} "a female cat" is [هِرَر]{.ar},
We will now see this role reversal of [ة]{.ar} in the numbers three to ten.
When a number from 3--10 is used for a masculine noun then it shall have [ة]{.ar}
and when it is used for a feminine noun then it shall not have [ة]{.ar}.
Here then are the numbers from 3--10:

| Number | Masculine | Feminine |
|:---|---:|---:|
| three  | [ثَلَاثَة]{.ar}   | [ثَلَاث]{.ar}   |
| four   | [أَرْبَعَة]{.ar}   | [أَرْبَع]{.ar}   |
| five   | [خَمْسَة]{.ar}    | [خَمْس]{.ar}    |
| six    | [سِتَّة]{.ar}     | [سِتّ]{.ar}     |
| seven  | [سَبْعَة]{.ar}    | [سَبْع]{.ar}    |
| eight  | [ثَمَانِيَة]{.ar}  | [ثَمَانِي]{.ar}  |
| nine   | [تِسْعَة]{.ar}    | [تِسْع]{.ar}    |
| ten    | [عَشَرَة]{.ar}    | [عَشْر]{.ar}    |

The number 10 is irregular in that the masculine number has a [fatHah]{.trn2} on the [ش]{.ar} 
([عَشَرَة]{.ar} [eacarah]{.trn}).
Whereas
the feminine number has a [sukUn]{.trn2} on the [ش]{.ar} 
([عَشْر]{.ar} [eacr]{.trn}).

Consistent with the numbers 1 and 2, the numbers from 3--10 may be used as attributes.
But they are more often used as annexed nouns.
As mentioned earlier, we will treat numbers more fully later, if [#allAh]{.trn2} wills.
But here are some examples for now:

[لِلْمَرْأَةِ ثَلَاثَةُ أَبْنَاءٍ وَأَرْبَعُ بَنَاتٍ.]{.ar}  
[lilmarEati valAvatu EabnAEin waEarbaeu banAt.]{.trn}  
"The woman has three sons and four daughters."

[وَضَعْتُ عَشَرَةَ أَقْلَامٍ فِي عَشْرِ حَقِيبَاتٍ.]{.ar}  
[waDaetu eacarata EaqlAmin fI eacri HaqIbAt.]{.trn}  
"I put ten pens in ten bags."

The feminine number [ثَمَانِي]{.ar} "eight" has a final weak letter [ي]{.ar}.
As we will learn in chapter\ (give ref), this final letter cannot take a [kasrah]{.trn2} or a [Dammah]{.trn2}.
For example,

[أَكَلَتْ ثَمَانِي هِرَرٍ ثَمَانِيَ كِسَرَاتٍ مِنَ ٱللَّحْمِ فِي ثَمَانِي سَاعَاتٍ.]{.ar}  
[Eakalat vamAnI hirarin vamAniya kisarAtin mina -llaHmi fI vamAnI sAeAt.]{.trn}  
"Eight cats~f~ ate eight pieces of meat in eight hours."

<!--
| [ثَلَاث]{.ar}
| [أَرْبَع]{.ar}
| [خَمْس]{.ar}
| [سِتّ]{.ar}
| [سَبْ]{.ar}
| [ثَمَانِي]{.ar}
| [تِسْع]{.ar}
| [عَشْر]{.ar}

| [ثَلَاثَة]{.ar} 
| [أَرْبَعَة]{.ar} 
| [خَمْسَة]{.ar} 
| [سِتَّة]{.ar} 
| [سَبَْة]{.ar} 
| [ثَمَانِيَة]{.ar} 
| [تِسْعَة]{.ar} 
| [عَشْرَة]{.ar} 
-->
